kingdoms.name: Empire of the Sun
kingdoms.description: The dominant power of the Midlands. Born from the old solar cults of the river valleys, the Empire claims divine right over all farmland between the great rivers. Its gilded banners fly over half the known world.
kingdoms.architecture: Colonnaded civic halls, golden-tiled temples, wide paved roads radiating from the capital

kingdoms.name: Anurian Kingdom
kingdoms.description: A proud mountain kingdom older than the Empire. The Anurians claim descent from the first men to carve roads through the high passes. They are grim, patient, and slow to forgive.
kingdoms.architecture: Granite keeps built into cliff faces, low arched bridges, slab-stone keeps without ornamentation

kingdoms.name: Thul-Gaur State
kingdoms.description: A small, militarised state surviving on discipline alone. Thul-Gaur villages look like fortresses — every homestead is a defensive position, every citizen a soldier. Their land is small but unconquerable.
kingdoms.architecture: Low stone palisades everywhere; markets surrounded by walls; all entrances are guard-checked

kingdoms.name: The Barony of Hath-Vek
kingdoms.description: An autocratic barony ruled by an iron-fisted council of barons. Power flows downward and obedience flows upward. The streets are clean because fear is a reliable janitor.
kingdoms.architecture: Tall stark watchtowers; public arenas for law-judgement; no ornamentation beyond rank insignia

kingdoms.name: The Gold Traders Guild
kingdoms.description: Not a nation by birth but by coin. The Guild holds title to roads, river crossings, and marketplaces across the Midlands. Their orange pennants fly wherever money changes hands — which is to say, everywhere.
kingdoms.architecture: Counting-houses, dockside warehouses, fortified trade halls with underground vaults

kingdoms.name: Luncratia
kingdoms.description: A loose federation of independent settlements that resist absorption by the larger powers. Luncratians prize local governance above all. No two towns agree on anything — and they like it that way.
kingdoms.stance: Fiercely independent, decentralised, democratic by instinct
kingdoms.architecture: Each town distinct; communal meeting halls, open market squares, no two settlements built the same

kingdoms.name: The Clerical State
kingdoms.description: A theocratic micro-state centred on a single great city of learning and worship. The Clerical State holds no armies, but its edicts carry the weight of divine sanction. Nations listen — or face the withdrawal of Materium rites.
kingdoms.stance: Neutral, authoritative on spiritual matters, diplomatically protected
kingdoms.architecture: White-stone temples, libraries, hospitals, and scriptoria surrounding a grand cathedral-keep

kingdoms.name: Rebel City States
kingdoms.description: Cities and towns that have broken from empire and barony alike. Each Rebel City State is its own sovereign — held together only by mutual treaties against conquest. They are loud, fractious, and impossible to fully extinguish.
kingdoms.stance: Revolutionary, fiercely sovereign, suspicious of all central authority
kingdoms.architecture: Barricaded gates, defiant civic monuments, revolutionary symbols painted on walls
